\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Appendix: Model Selection History}
\chapter*{Appendix: Model Selection History}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{A.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{A.\arabic{figure}}
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
This appendix consolidates every configuration explored during model
selection, so that any choice made in the body of the thesis can be traced
to the attempt that motivated it. Two records are given. Table~\ref{tab:appfamily}
is the detector-family benchmark that fixed the YOLO11m baseline; it was run
under an earlier protocol (25 epochs on a $50\%$ data sample, batch~10, on
different hardware), so its absolute values are not comparable to the final
ablation of Table~\ref{tab:main} and
it serves only to justify the backbone choice; $P$, $R$, and $F_1$ follow
the definitions of Section~\ref{sec:metrics}, and Size is the weight-file
size. Table~\ref{tab:apphistory}
then lists every architectural variant attempted across the project with its
outcome and the decision it produced, including the attempts that failed and
the one whose first results were invalidated by an implementation fault.

\begin{table}[H]
  \centering
  \caption{Detector-family benchmark used to select the baseline.}
  \label{tab:appfamily}
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}l r r r r r r@{}}
    \toprule
    \textbf{Model} & \textbf{mAP50} & \textbf{mAP50-95} & \textbf{$\symbf{P}$} & \textbf{$\symbf{R}$} & \textbf{$\symbf{F_1}$} & \textbf{Size (MB)} \\
    \midrule
    YOLOv9-s   & 0.787 & 0.504 & 0.841 & 0.736 & 0.785 & 14.5 \\
    YOLOv9-m   & 0.823 & 0.565 & 0.863 & 0.772 & 0.815 & 38.9 \\
    YOLOv9-e   & 0.855 & 0.619 & 0.884 & 0.805 & 0.843 & 111.8 \\
    YOLOv10-s  & 0.792 & 0.517 & 0.836 & 0.738 & 0.784 & 15.8 \\
    YOLOv10-m  & 0.827 & 0.571 & 0.857 & 0.777 & 0.815 & 31.9 \\
    YOLOv10-l  & 0.843 & 0.598 & 0.870 & 0.792 & 0.829 & 49.8 \\
    YOLO11-s   & 0.803 & 0.535 & 0.845 & 0.757 & 0.798 & 18.3 \\
    \textbf{YOLO11m (selected)} & 0.841 & 0.592 & 0.867 & 0.794 & 0.829 & 38.6 \\
    YOLO11-l   & 0.847 & 0.603 & 0.876 & 0.796 & 0.834 & 48.8 \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

The choice of backbone was an accuracy-against-cost trade-off, and
Figure~\ref{fig:appfrontier} plots the same benchmark that way, with detection
accuracy on the vertical axis and weight-file size, a proxy for parameter
count, on the horizontal. The nine detectors fall along a rising curve of
diminishing returns. YOLO11m sits on the efficient part of that curve: at
$38.6$~MB it reaches an mAP@50--95 of $0.592$, within $0.03$ of the largest
model (YOLOv9-e) which needs roughly three times the size, and it matches or
beats every detector of comparable size. That balance of accuracy and
deployable footprint is why YOLO11m was fixed as the baseline for the ablation
in the body.

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
  \begin{tikzpicture}
    \begin{axis}[
      width=0.86\linewidth, height=6.6cm,
      xlabel={Weight-file size (MB) $\rightarrow$ proxy for parameter count},
      ylabel={mAP@50--95},
      xmin=0, xmax=120, ymin=0.48, ymax=0.64,
      grid=both, grid style={gray!18},
      legend pos=south east,
      legend style={font=\footnotesize, draw=gray!50, fill=white},
      tick label style={font=\footnotesize},
      label style={font=\small},
      every axis plot/.append style={mark size=2.6pt},
    ]
      \addplot[only marks, mark=*, color=blue!70!black]
        coordinates {(14.5,0.504) (38.9,0.565) (111.8,0.619)};
      \addlegendentry{YOLOv9 (s,m,e)}
      \addplot[only marks, mark=square*, color=orange!85!black]
        coordinates {(15.8,0.517) (31.9,0.571) (49.8,0.598)};
      \addlegendentry{YOLOv10 (s,m,l)}
      \addplot[only marks, mark=triangle*, color=teal]
        coordinates {(18.3,0.535) (48.8,0.603)};
      \addlegendentry{YOLO11 (s,l)}
      \addplot[only marks, mark=star, mark size=5pt, color=red]
        coordinates {(38.6,0.592)};
      \addlegendentry{YOLO11m (selected)}
      \node[font=\scriptsize, anchor=west] at (axis cs:113,0.619) {v9-e};
      \node[font=\scriptsize, anchor=south] at (axis cs:38.6,0.598) {11m};
    \end{axis}
  \end{tikzpicture}
  \caption{Detection accuracy against model size for the nine benchmarked detectors of Table~\ref{tab:appfamily}; size stands in for parameter count. YOLO11m (star) lies on the efficient frontier.}
  \label{fig:appfrontier}
\end{figure}

Table~\ref{tab:apphistory} is the second record: every architectural variant
tried after the baseline was fixed, with the outcome it produced and the
decision it drove. It is kept so that the negative results carry the same weight
as the positive ones. Three patterns are worth drawing out. The two components
that survived, CBAM and the P2 head, were retained because each showed a
measurable gain on the metric it targeted rather than on the aggregate. The
state-space neck was attempted twice: the first run was silently invalidated by
a framework fault that stripped the injected blocks, which is why a
module-presence check was later added to the protocol, and the corrected run
returned a genuine null. The AtrousSSM prototype was set aside before a full run
because its smoke tests showed no reliable gain. Read together, the table
records not only what was adopted but the cost of finding it out, which is the
part of a project that a results chapter usually omits.

\begin{table}[H]
  \centering
  \caption{Architectural variants attempted during the project, with outcome and decision.}
  \label{tab:apphistory}
  \small
  \setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
  \begin{tabular}{@{}p{2.9cm} p{4.3cm} p{4.4cm} p{2.4cm}@{}}
    \toprule
    \textbf{Variant} & \textbf{What it changes} & \textbf{Outcome} & \textbf{Decision} \\
    \midrule
    ECA attention & C2PSA $\to$ ECA in backbone & $F_2$ 0.844, small-object recall 0.884 (earlier protocol): no gain over baseline & not adopted \\
    CBAM attention & C2PSA $\to$ CBAM in backbone & $F_2$ 0.849, small-object recall 0.892 (earlier protocol); confirmed in final chain (Table~\ref{tab:main}) & adopted \\
    P2 detection scale & adds stride-4 head branch & $\mathrm{AP}_{50}$ $0.843 \to 0.853$, very-tiny recall $0.743 \to 0.757$ (final protocol) & adopted; core of recommended model \\
    C3k2Mamba, first attempt & post-initialisation SSM injection into neck & results invalid: the framework's training rebuild silently removed the injected blocks, so the model trained was CBAM~+~P2 & discarded; protocol gained module-presence verification \\
    AtrousSSM & multi-scale dilated SSM scanning in neck & prototype stage; smoke evaluations showed no reliable gain and no protocol-compliant full run was completed & set aside \\
    C3k2Mamba, corrected & verified SSM blocks at six neck layers & genuine null: $+2.4$~M parameters, $2.8\times$ latency, no accuracy change (Table~\ref{tab:main}); scan-activity diagnostics confirm the blocks ran & excluded from recommendation \\
    \bottomrule
  \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Finally, a note on reproducibility. Every run recorded the artifacts needed to
repeat it. Training used a single fixed seed (0) with deterministic cuDNN
kernels; the train, validation, and test splits are pinned by content hash so
that no image crosses between them; and each run wrote an environment manifest
with the library versions, the dataset and weight-file hashes, and the full
hyperparameter set. The body's final ablation ran on the RTX~4070~Ti~SUPER of
Section~\ref{sec:setup}, whereas the family benchmark of
Table~\ref{tab:appfamily} predates that machine and used the shorter protocol
noted above, which is why its absolute scores are offset from the body's. These
records let the negative results here be reported as measurements, not
impressions.
